status_at_import: 21 articles and 4 tools merged via scrabble-enhancements modules.

primary_queries: Scrabble two letter words FAQ QI ZA JO valid current official dictionary
Scrabble bingo strategy rack balance common bingo stems FAQ
Words With Friends scoring tile values bingo bonus FAQ
Scrabble Q words without U QI QAT QOPH FAQ
related_search_intents: check whether a short odd word is legal
find high-scoring short plays for a difficult rack
understand how bingos, blanks, and premium squares affect score
compare Scrabble and Words With Friends scoring
learn Q-without-U, X, Z, and J emergency vocabulary
serp_research.notes: SERP patterns were used to shape PAA-style FAQs and intent coverage; Kefiw implementation details remain the source of truth for feature claims.

adsense_readiness.original_utility: Strong: the page is attached to an interactive word-game utility or a practical guide, not just scraped definitions.
adsense_readiness.content_depth: Tool pages should stay concise; guide and list pages need the longform body plus FAQs to avoid thin-content risk.
ad_placement_notes: Do not place ads between the page title and the primary input or word list.
Keep sticky or anchor ads from covering rack inputs, filters, result tables, or copy buttons.
For long guides, place ads after substantial content sections rather than immediately after every heading.
adsense_readiness.policy_sensitive_claims: Low. Position helpers as learning, validation, and casual-game aids; avoid implying tournament certification or encouraging rule-breaking.
trust_notes: Explain that Kefiw uses ENABLE1 rather than an official tournament dictionary.
Flag blank-tile scoring limitations where relevant.
Avoid claiming full board modelling unless the implementation adds it later.
adsense_readiness.article_page_specific: cluster content is suitable for contextual ads after the first worked example and near FAQ, not inside tables or lists.

audit_notes.preserved_source_fields: All id, slug, url, title, h1, current, relatedIds, and link_map values were preserved from the V2 file.

corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.issue: The live tool currently scores blanks as full tile value, while real Scrabble blanks score zero.
corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.enhanced_handling: Enhanced limitations, FAQ, tips, and suggested enhancements tell users to verify blank-involved scores and propose zero-value blank scoring.

corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.issue: The live WWF helper has the same blank-scoring overcount as the Scrabble helper.
corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.enhanced_handling: Enhanced copy avoids claiming exact real-game blank scores and proposes a blank-value fix.

corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.issue: The current outcome line says pattern matching supports *; verified logic supports only single-letter wildcards ?, _, and . in Pattern mode.
corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.enhanced_handling: Enhanced outcome line and FAQ describe exact-length pattern matching with single-character wildcards only.

corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.issue: The source contains conflicting guidance about whether JO is valid; Kefiw/ENABLE-related pages list JO as a playable short J word.
corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.enhanced_handling: Enhanced copy states that JO appears in the Kefiw list but users should confirm unusual words in their game dictionary.

corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.issue: QI is shown as 12 in one current example, but QI is 11 using standard Scrabble values.
corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.enhanced_handling: Enhanced examples and FAQs use QI = 11 and explain raw score versus board multipliers.

corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.issue: Several current notes imply board-state or hook-position modelling, but the verified Scrabble/WWF helpers only support an optional single through-letter.
corrected_in_enhanced_not_current.enhanced_handling: Enhanced tool references describe rack search and optional through-letter filtering, not full placement solving.

high_priority_product_improvements: Score blanks as zero and show which letter each blank represents.
Add board modelling for premium squares, cross-checks, hooks, and parallel plays.
Add dictionary-mode labels or toggles for ENABLE1, official Scrabble/NWL-style play, Collins-style play, and Words With Friends if licensing permits.
Add word-list tables generated from the same dictionary powering each list page, with score and length filters.

existing_features_to_preserve: Client-side word lookup for fast interaction
Shared ENABLE1 source for consistent game-list behavior
Score-aware helpers for Scrabble and WWF
Internal linking between tools, lists, and guides

proposed_features_ranked.feature: Zero-value blank scoring across every scoring surface
proposed_features_ranked.user_benefit: Restores trust in score-ranked results whenever ? is used.

proposed_features_ranked.feature: Full board solver layer for Scrabble and WWF helpers
proposed_features_ranked.user_benefit: Turns candidate words into legal board placements with premium-square and cross-word scoring.

proposed_features_ranked.feature: Dictionary transparency controls and validation badges
proposed_features_ranked.user_benefit: Users can see whether a result is from ENABLE1, a broader list, or a future licensed game dictionary.

proposed_features_ranked.feature: Generated word-list tables with score, length, and starts/ends filters
proposed_features_ranked.user_benefit: List pages become more original and useful for AdSense review and user retention.

proposed_features_ranked.feature: Word detail drawer with definition, hooks, plural, score, and source-list note
proposed_features_ranked.user_benefit: Users learn why a strange word is playable and can defend it in casual games.

user_scenarios: A casual player has seven tiles and wants to avoid missing a high-value short play.
A learner is drilling Q-without-U and X words from real racks.
A player sees one open board letter and wants words that can pass through it.
A content reader moves from a strategy guide into a live rack check.
tips: Compare the best short play and best long play; a short Z or X play can beat a longer low-value word.
Use the through-letter field sparingly. It means the word must contain that letter, not that the tool knows the board layout.
When a blank creates several bingos, test the real board before spending it on a small score.
After choosing a candidate, check cross-words and premium squares in the game itself.

proposed_features.feature: Correct blank scoring to zero and display the substituted blank letter.
proposed_features.user_benefit: Users can trust score totals when ? is used and can see how the word is being formed.

proposed_features.feature: Add full board placement with premium squares, cross-checks, hooks, and parallel words.
proposed_features.user_benefit: Users can move from rack candidates to legal board plays without manual geometry checks.

proposed_features.feature: Add dictionary-mode labels or toggles where licensing allows.
proposed_features.user_benefit: Users can see whether they are practicing ENABLE1, Scrabble-style, Collins-style, or WWF-style vocabulary.

proposed_features.feature: Add explanations for unusual words on tap.
proposed_features.user_benefit: Users learn vocabulary instead of copying opaque suggestions.

proposed_features.feature: Add export filters for length, score band, and contains high-value tile.
proposed_features.user_benefit: Users can turn results into study lists quickly.

user_scenarios: A WWF player has a strong rack but cannot tell whether the Scrabble answer is still best.
A learner wants a safe way to study common short WWF plays.
A player is trying to dump a Q or J without destroying next-turn balance.
A strategy reader wants to see how the same rack differs between games.
tips: Recheck any rack with J, C, B, L, N, U, or Y because WWF values differ from Scrabble.
Use the score gap to decide whether a risky top play is worth opening the board.
If several plays are close, prefer the one with a stronger leave or safer board shape.
Use the Scrabble helper only when the game is actually Scrabble.

user_scenarios: A puzzle solver has mixed letters and needs every possible word by length.
A Scrabble learner is studying bingos from common stems.
A classroom user wants examples of words made from the same letters.
A player wants to see whether a blank creates a usable word family.
tips: Start with a lower minimum length if the rack is awkward, then raise it once you see useful stems.
Use Game list for Scrabble-like practice and Full list for broader word discovery.
Turn on scores only after you have scanned the longest words, so score does not distract from pattern recognition.
Move to the Scrabble helper when you are ready to rank a rack by game score.

user_scenarios: A puzzle solver knows the second and fourth letters but not the rest.
A word-game player wants all words from a rack between 4 and 7 letters.
A learner is drilling fixed patterns like ??ING or A?E??.
A list-page reader wants to move from study to a custom search.
tips: Use Pattern mode when fixed positions matter; it is faster than scanning a long word list manually.
Use Letters mode for racks where the exact position does not matter.
Switch from Full list to Game list when results feel too obscure for word games.
Move to the Scrabble helper when score ranking is more important than pattern matching.
